title: Transients & vibrations
summary: Transient machine dynamics (clutch engagement, motor-load start, reduction of inertia and torque to one shaft), flywheel sizing for periodic steady state, and single-DOF vibrations: equivalent stiffness, free undamped and damped oscillations, forced response, resonance and base-excitation transmissibility.
objectives: Reduce a motor-clutch-reducer-load chain to one shaft (reduced inertia J* and reduced torque) and integrate the Newton equation J*dω/dt = C
Solve a clutch engagement transient: slipping torque C*, common speed ω*, slip time t*, dissipated heat Q
Size a flywheel for periodic steady state: average-torque balance, surplus energy, fluctuation index i and ω_max/ω_min
Build equivalent stiffness for springs in series/parallel and for elements acting through a lever arm, and find ω_n = √(k/m)
Characterize free damped motion: damping ratio ζ, ω_d, and back out a damping coefficient from a required ζ
Compute steady-state forced response (amplitude and phase) for direct forcing and for imposed base motion (transmissibility)

blocks.text:
This topic gathers the machine-dynamics problems that are not pure statics or kinematics: a system goes through a **transient** (a clutch engages, a motor spins up a load), or it **oscillates**. Three ideas cover almost everything:

- **Reduction to one shaft.** Replace the whole motor-clutch-reducer-load chain with one equivalent rotor: a reduced inertia $J^*$ and a reduced torque, then write a single equation $J^*\dot\omega = C^*$.
- **Periodic steady state / flywheel.** Over one cycle the average motor torque equals the resistant torque; the flywheel absorbs the surplus energy and limits the speed ripple.
- **Single-DOF vibration.** Any spring-mass-damper (even a pivoted bar) reduces to $J\ddot q + c_{eq}\dot q + k_{eq}q = $ forcing. From there everything follows from $\omega_n=\sqrt{k/m}$, the damping ratio $\zeta$, resonance and transmissibility.

Pick a reference shaft (usually the motor). A body on a shaft running at speed ratio $\tau=\omega/\omega_{ref}$ contributes an **equivalent inertia** $J\,\tau^2$ to the reference shaft; a translating mass $m$ moving at $v=\omega_{ref}\rho$ contributes $m\rho^2$. A torque $C$ on that shaft reflects as $C\,\tau$. The whole machine then obeys one equation $J^*\dot\omega_{ref}=C_{motor}-C_{res}^*$.

Because $\tau<1$ for a speed reducer, the big drum/load inertia is divided by the (large) reduction squared as seen from the motor — that is why a small motor can start a heavy load. When the motor torque is **linear in speed**, $C_M=C_0(1-\omega/\omega_{max})$, the reduced equation becomes first-order linear and the speed grows exponentially: $\omega(t)=\omega_\infty\,(1-e^{-t/\mathcal{T}})$ with time constant $\mathcal{T}=J^*/(C_0/\omega_{max})$.
During engagement the two sides **slip**, so the clutch transmits a constant friction torque $C^*$ (uniform-wear assumption, mean radius $r_m=(r_i+r_e)/2$, $N$ friction surfaces):

Motor side decelerates, $J_M\dot\omega_M = C_M-C^*$; driven side accelerates, $J_D\dot\omega_D=C^*-C_D$. Slipping ends when $\omega_M=\omega_D=\omega^*$. After lock-up the two rotate together: $(J_M+J_D)\dot\omega=C_M-C_D$.

Heat in a clutch = transmitted torque × **relative** slip angle. Since the relative speed falls linearly from $\omega_{M0}$ to $0$ over $t^*$, the slip angle is the triangle area $\tfrac12\,\omega_{M0}\,t^*$. Convert with $1\text{ kcal}=4186\text{ J}$.

Over one full cycle the speed returns to its starting value, so the net work is zero: the **average** motor torque equals the constant resistant torque, $\overline{C_M}=C_R=P/\omega_m$. Within the cycle the instantaneous torques differ; the surplus/deficit energy $\Delta E$ is stored in and released by the flywheel, producing a speed ripple.

$\Delta E$ is the area between the $C_M(\theta)$ curve and the horizontal line $C_R$ over the part of the cycle where the engine torque exceeds the resistance. Annular flywheel inertia: $I=\tfrac12 m(R_e^2+R_i^2)$ with $m=\rho\,\pi(R_e^2-R_i^2)\,s$.

When a spring (stiffness $k$) acts at distance $d$ from a pivot while you write the equation in the angle $\theta$, its **rotational** stiffness is $k\,d^2$. Likewise a damper $c$ at arm $b$ gives $c\,b^2$, and a force $F$ at arm $a$ gives a torque $F a$. A spring attached at the top of a rolling disc (arm $2r$ from the contact point) referred to the centre behaves like $4k$.

For $J\ddot q + c_{eq}\dot q + k_{eq}q=0$: natural frequency $\omega_n=\sqrt{k_{eq}/J}$, damping ratio $\zeta=\dfrac{c_{eq}}{2\sqrt{k_{eq}J}}$, damped frequency $\omega_d=\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$, logarithmic decrement $\delta=\ln\dfrac{q_n}{q_{n+1}}=\dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
For $J\ddot\theta + c_{eq}\dot\theta + k_{eq}\theta = M_0\sin\omega t$, the steady-state amplitude and phase lag are:

If the excitation is an **imposed base motion** $x=x_0\sin\omega t$ entering through the spring, replace $M_0$ by $k_{eq}x_0$ (use $k\,a\,x_0$ if the spring sits at arm $a$). For a mass on a spring+damper driven by a moving base $y$ (suspension on a road), the transmitted amplitude follows the **transmissibility**:

Classic mistakes: (1) forgetting $N$ friction surfaces or using $r_e$ instead of the mean radius $r_m$ in clutch torque; (2) reflecting inertia with $\tau$ instead of $\tau^2$; (3) using peak-to-peak road height as the amplitude — the amplitude is **half** the peak-to-peak ($y_0=Y_s/2$); (4) in resonance/forced problems, writing $\varphi=\arctan(\dots)$ without checking the quadrant — when $\omega>\omega_n$ the denominator $k-J\omega^2$ is negative and $\varphi>90^\circ$ (use atan2).

steps.label: Reduce to one DOF
steps.text: For drivelines compute $J^*$ (inertia $\times\tau^2$, mass $\times\rho^2$) and reduced torque ($\times\tau$). For levered springs/dampers use arm-squared: $k a^2$, $c b^2$.

steps.label: Write ONE equation
steps.text: Newton/Euler: $J^*\dot\omega=C_{motor}-C_{res}^*$ for transients, or $J\ddot q+c_{eq}\dot q+k_{eq}q=$ forcing for vibration.

steps.label: Solve the standard form
steps.text: Constant torque → linear ramp; linear motor torque → exponential with $\mathcal T=J^*/(C_0/\omega_{max})$; vibration → $\omega_n,\ \zeta,\ \omega_d$ or the forced amplitude/phase formulas.

steps.label: Energy / heat / fluctuation
steps.text: Clutch heat $=C^*\times$ slip angle; flywheel $i=\Delta E/(I\omega_m^2)$.

steps.label: Sanity-check units & quadrant
steps.text: rad/s, N, Nm, J; convert kcal with 4186 J; use atan2 for the phase.

question.prompt: A drum of inertia $J_D$ on a shaft running at $\tau=\omega_{out}/\omega_{in}=1/5$ is reflected to the motor shaft. Its equivalent inertia there is:
question.explanation: Inertia reflects with the square of the speed ratio: $J_D\tau^2 = J_D(1/5)^2 = J_D/25$. (Torque, by contrast, reflects linearly with $\tau$.)

question.prompt: A wheel rides over a sinusoidal road of wavelength $L_0$ at speed $v$. The excitation angular frequency of the vertical motion is:
question.explanation: The wheel meets one full wave every $L_0/v$ seconds, so the temporal frequency is $f=v/L_0$ and $\omega=2\pi f = 2\pi v/L_0$. The road amplitude is half the peak-to-peak height.
